title: Ratings Quality and Borrowing Choice
abstract: Past studies document that incentive conflicts may lead issuer-paid credit rating agencies to provide optimistically biased ratings. In this paper, we present evidence that investors question the quality of issuer-paid ratings and raise corporate bond yields where the issuer-paid rating is more positive than benchmark investor-paid ratings. We also find that some firms with favorable issuer-paid ratings substitute public bonds with borrowings from informed intermediaries to mitigate the “lemons discount” associated with poor quality ratings. Overall, our results suggest that the quality of issuer-paid ratings has significant effects on borrowing costs and the choice of debt.

article.introduction:
Credit rating agencies are financialintermediaries that facilitate ‘‘arm's length” debt financing by reducing information asymmetries between issuers and investors through forward-looking opinions about the credit quality of issuers and their specific debt obligations. By aggregating information about credit quality from public and private sources into a single measure, rating agencies reduce individual investors’ need to conduct detailed due diligence and thereby broaden issuers’ investor base beyond sophisticated investors willing and able to engage in complex credit analyses (see, for example, Faulkender and Petersen (2006) and Sufi (2009)).
Past studies suggest, however, that reliance on compensation paid by the issuers that they rate may lead rating agencies to provide over optimistic ratings.1In this paper, we present evidence that investors treat issuer-paid ratings with skepticism (question their quality), raising corporate bond yields when the issuer-paid rating is more favorable than the investor-paid rating, which induces some firms to substitute public bonds with intermediated debt.
To motivate our empirical analysis, we construct a simple model of ratings inflation. In our model, there are two rating agencies: one whose fees are paid by issuers and that has a propensity to inflatesomeratings, and another whose fees are paid by investors and that does not inflate its ratings. The model illustrates how inflation in issuer-paid ratings can exacerbate information asymmetries even when investors recognize and attempt to price it.
Our primary empirical measure of ratings quality is based on the difference between Standard & Poor's (S&P) and Egan-Jones Ratings Company's (EJR) firm-level senior debt ratings. We define a firm as overrated (underrated) if S&P rates the issuer more (less) favorably than EJR. We compare S&P ratings, which are issuer-paid, to investor-paid EJR ratings because EJR uses the same long-term rating scale, defines its ratings in the same way as S&P, and, like S&P, provides through-the-cycle ratings based on default probabilities.2In robustness tests, we also measure ratings quality by comparing Moody's ratings to CDS-implied ratings.3
Of course, ratings disagreements between S&P and EJR may arise simply because of differences in quantitative credit risk models or qualitative risk assessments, and thus, S&P overrating (relative to EJR) does not necessarily indicate ratings inflation. However, using five sets of empirical tests, we find evidence that S&P overrating reflects, in part, ratings inflation by S&P and not just genuine differences of opinion between the two agencies.
We begin by documenting that, within an S&P rating category, firms that are overrated by S&P are riskier—based on both ex-ante firm financial characteristics and ex-post default rates—than both firms that are overrated by EJR and firms that are rated the same by the two agencies. In contrast, we find no significant difference in the default rates of firms that are overrated by EJR (i.e., underrated by S&P) and those that are rated the same by S&P and EJR. These results indicate that EJR ratings add to the granularity of S&P ratings, but only when S&P is the more optimistic agency. This asymmetry is hard to explain in the absence of incentive conflicts, and is consistent with S&P overrating serving as an indicator of poor ratings quality.
Next, we demonstrate that the likelihood of S&P overrating is positively related to commonly used empirical proxies for the severity of the conflicts of interest faced by issuer-paid rating agencies. For example, we find that the frequency of S&P overrating is significantly greater for issuers that contribute more to S&P's rating revenues (e.g., frequent bond issuers and issuers with many outstanding bond issues) as well as issuers that have greater incentives to engage in ratings shopping (e.g., high-yield [HY] issuers and issuers at the boundary between HY and investment-grade [IG] ratings).
We also examine the time-series evolution of S&P and EJR ratings after EJR first initiates coverage of an issuer. We find that S&P is more likely (and faster) to converge toward EJR when it is initially the more optimistic agency than when the two agencies agree or when S&P is the more pessimistic agency. We do not find a similar pattern for EJR ratings. These results suggest that S&P overrating does not arise simply from structural differences in the way the two rating agencies assign ratings to issuers. Rather, the evidence is more consistent with optimistic S&P ratings being less timely and thus of lower quality than benchmark EJR ratings.
Finally, to establish a causal connection between conflicts of interest and S&P overrating, we exploit a quasi-random shock to S&P's incentives to engage in ratings inflation. Fong et al. (2014) argue that exogenous reductions in a firm's sell-side analyst coverage due to brokerage firm mergers (or closures) give issuer-paid rating agencies greater leeway to engage in ratings inflation without being detected. Their argument is that consensus earnings forecasts are, on average, less optimistic and more accurate when more sell-side analysts cover an issuer, which, in turn, should make it more difficult for issuer-paid rating agencies to issue optimistically biased ratings without losing credibility. Based on this insight, we investigate changes in the likelihood of S&P overrating following brokerage firm closures or mergers. Using a difference-in-differences estimation strategy, we document economically large and statistically significant increases in the likelihood of S&P overrating following brokerage firm mergers or closures for firms affected by these events relative to a control sample that consists of firms that have the same S&P and EJR ratings as the affected firms but that are unaffected by such events. Taken together, these results provide strong support for the argument that S&P overrating is a valid measure of poor ratings quality.
After validating our ratings quality measure, we examine whether investors recognize and price poor-quality issuer-paid ratings. We focus on bond prices in secondary markets to avoid potential selection issues.4We employ two identification strategies. The first strategy is motivated by our model of ratings optimism in which investors base their investment decisions on observed ratings by EJR and S&P. In particular, it is based on comparing the yields associated with symmetric split-ratings pairs. We limit the ratings disagreements to just one notch, such that the average rating between symmetric split-ratings pairs is the same. For example, we compare the yield (YO) for a bond rated BB+ by S&P and BB by EJR to the yield (YP) for a bond rated BB by S&P and BB+ by EJR. The yield equalsYOwhen S&P is more optimistic than EJR andYPwhen S&P is more pessimistic than EJR. Our model assumes that in the absence of ratings inflation, S&P and EJR ratings are, on average, equally informative about future defaults, in which case investors will weigh them equally and the yields of the two split-rated bonds will be the same:YO=YP. If, in contrast, S&P has a propensity to inflate ratings but investors are unable to identify which firms received inflated ratings, investors will place less weight on optimistic S&P ratings than on pessimistic S&P ratings such thatYO>YP. Additionally, if the difference betweenYOandYPreflects ratings quality, we would expect the difference to be larger when S&P's incentives to inflate ratings are likely to be greater.
Consistent with investors incorporating the quality of S&P ratings into bond prices, we find that when S&P ratings are more optimistic than EJR ratings, investors assign bonds a ‘‘lemons discount” in the form of a yield premium relative to bonds in which S&P is more pessimistic than EJR. The yield premium is significant only at the HY-IG boundary and for HY bonds, for which ratings inflation can have an economically more meaningful effect on borrowing costs. For HY bonds, the premium associated with S&P optimism is, on average, about 54 basis points greater than the premium associated with EJR optimism. At the HY-IG boundary, the premium is about 34 basis points. Also, the estimated impact of ratings quality on bond spreads is greater when S&P's incentives to issue inflated ratings are likely to be greatest. For example, for HY bonds, the premium associated with S&P optimism is, on average, about 90 basis points for frequent issuers and for firms with the largest number of bonds outstanding (firms that contribute most to S&P's corporate rating revenues). Furthermore, for frequent issuers and issuers with many outstanding bonds, the premium is about 62 basis points, on average, around the HY-IG boundary. Finally, we find no significant premium for HY issuers or for firms at the HY-IG boundary that are infrequent issuers with few outstanding bonds.
There are two potential concerns about these reduced-form pricing results. First, there may be unobserved heterogeneity in the credit risk characteristics of firms that are overrated and those that are not overrated by S&P. Second, if EJR provides systematically more accurate ratings than S&P, then when there is disagreement between the two agencies, investors will place greater weight on EJR ratings even in the absence of ratings inflation. We address both of these concerns using an identification strategy based on the quasi-natural experiment used in our validation tests. Specifically, we use brokerage closures as an instrument for S&P overrating and find higher yields for overrated firms with HY ratings and for overrated firms at the HY-IG boundary.
Next, we examine whether ratings quality affects firms’ choice between private debt and “arm's length” debt. Our model illustrates that poor-quality ratings will result in firms with diverse risks being pooled together, which, in turn, will exacerbate information asymmetries and result in misvaluations in the bond market.5If private lenders are able to alleviate information asymmetries, for example, by performing extensive due diligence, conditional on having a positive demand for debt, firms whose bonds are undervalued because of poor-quality ratings will substitute private debt from informed intermediaries for public debt. The empirical implication is that the proportion of firms that find borrowing from private sources more attractive than borrowing from public markets will be greatest among issuers overrated by S&P.
To test this implication, we identify movements in the bond supply due to mispricing using the empirical approach of Becker and Ivashina (2014). Specifically, we examine the substitution between private debt and public debt conditional on the issuance of new debt. By revealed preferences, firms that obtain new debt financing must have a positive demand for debt. In the context of our model, we interpret a substitution of private debt for public debt by firms that are overrated by S&P as evidence of a shift in the bond supply based on poor-quality ratings. Consistent with a shift in bond supply, we find that, relative to firms that are not overrated by S&P, firms overrated by S&P are significantly more likely to borrow from private debt markets than from the public bond market.
For our final set of tests, we examine whether borrowing from well-informed intermediaries can alleviate the asymmetric information discount associated with poor-quality ratings. We would expect borrowing from private lenders (e.g., banks) to reveal positive private information about the credit quality of the issuer or to provide cross-monitoring benefits, therefore reducing the premium in bond spreads associated with low-quality ratings. To test this prediction, we examine changes in bond spreads around debt offerings for overrated and non-overrated firms. We find that the spread premium associated with S&P overrating is significantly reduced following private debt issuances, consistent with issuances of informed debt mitigating the “lemons discount” in bond prices.
Our paper is related to several strands of the literature on corporate credit ratings. First, similar to recent papers by Beaver, Shakespeare, and Soliman (2006), Cornaggia and Cornaggia (2013), and Xia (2014), we investigate the informativeness of issuer-paid and investor-paid ratings. Both Beaver, Shakespeare, and Soliman (2006) and Cornaggia and Cornaggia (2013) find that changes in investor-paid ratings are more timely. In addition, Bruno, Cornaggia, and Cornaggia (2015) find that differences in the timeliness of Moody's and EJR ratings persist after EJR is designated an NRSRO in December 2007, suggesting that differences between EJR and Moody's do not arise from regulatory certification. Finally, Xia (2014) finds that the introduction of EJR ratings increases the sensitivity of S&P ratings to innovations in credit risk. Overall, these papers use investor-paid EJR ratings to assess the quality of issuer-paid ratings and find evidence consistent with ratings inflation. Like these papers, we use EJR ratings to assess the quality of S&P ratings. Our analysis focuses on the impact of ratings disagreements on bond spreads in secondary markets. We show that when there is a readily available, low-cost way for investors to evaluate the quality of credit ratings, investors require a yield premium for bonds with poor-quality ratings.6
Second, our paper is related to recent work concerning the impact of ratings quality on debt issuance decisions. For example, Harford, Martos-Vila, and Rhodes-Kropf (2015) examine the relation between debt issuances and aggregate ratings optimism. They find that ratings optimism is related to bond spread deviations from expected default risk, which they interpret as evidence that bond prices do not fully reflect Moody's optimism. Harford, Martos-Vila, and Rhodes-Kropf's (2015) analysis focuses primarily on time-series variation in an ex-post measure of ratings quality over the period 1984 to 2004. We also examine the effect of ratings quality on bond prices and issuance decisions but focus on cross-sectional variation in ex-ante measures of ratings quality during a period in which S&P and Moody's optimism is arguably easier for market participants to identify and price. We find that poor-quality ratings are associated with an information gap that leads some overrated firms to be mispriced and that these mispriced firms substitute private debt for “arm's length” debt.
Finally, our paper is related to recent research on the impact of information asymmetries on security design. Specifically, Garleanu and Zwiebel (2009) and Dessein (2005) predict that increases in information asymmetries and the cost of becoming informed will result in borrowers ceding more control rights to lenders through covenants. Our finding that poor ratings quality is associated with an increase in the likelihood of issuing relatively covenant-heavy intermediated debt is consistent with the predictions of these studies.
The remainder of this paper is structured as follows. SectionIoutlines a model that illustrates how ratings quality can affect bond yields and issuers’ borrowing choices. SectionIIvalidates our measure of ratings quality. SectionIIIexamines whether ratings quality is reflected in secondary bond market spreads. SectionIVexamines whether ratings quality affects firms’ choice between private debt and “arm's length” debt. Finally, SectionVconcludes.
article.I. A Simple Model of Ratings Quality:
In this section, we outline our model that illustrates how the quality of credit ratings can affect bond yields, as well as issuers’ choice between private debt and “arm's length” public debt. We use the model to show how bond investors interpret differences between issuer- and investor-paid ratings when the issuer-paid agency has a propensity to engage in ratings inflation. Details concerning the model as well as all relevant proofs are presented in AppendixA. We begin this section by describing the setup of our model and how split ratings are related to bond yields in the absence of ratings inflation. We then show, in the context of our model, how ratings inflation affects bond yields and the choice between public and private debt.

Also, assume that the likelihood that a firm with credit riskiis assigned the correct rating is ρ, withρ≥13. Given these assumptions, rating agencies’ estimates of default risk are unbiased (i.e.,E[εi,S]=E[εi,E]=0).
Assume that bond investors rely on credit ratings to assess the probability of default, and that they update their assessment of the probability of default using Bayes's rule. If incentive conflicts lead one of the rating agencies to misreport some portion of its ratings, then investors’ inferences about credit risk will depend on their beliefs concerning the proportion of firms with misreported ratings. Consider the case in which the issuer-paid agency inflates its ratings by one notch for some firms based on factors unobservable to investors, such as future business opportunities. Investors know thatSsometimes issues inflated ratings, and they may believe that its propensity to inflate is related to observable firm characteristics, but they do not know whether the rating for a given firm is inflated. For example, investors may believe that frequent past issuers or firms at the HY-IG boundary are more likely to be assigned optimistic ratings byS, but they do not know exactly which firms have inflated ratings.

The model also shows how ratings quality affects the choice between public debt (or “arm's length” debt) and private debt. Firms may borrow from informed private lenders for a variety of reasons, including ease in recontracting, access to liquidity when needed, and lower adverse selection costs due to more extensive due diligence. In our model, we focus on adverse selection costs. Private lenders rely on public information as well as private information generated from extensive due diligence and ongoing monitoring. As a result, private lenders are able to reduce adverse selection problems by generating a more precise measure of firms’ credit risk. In addition, we assume that there is some incremental contracting cost of intermediated debt relative to public debt such that in the absence of adverse selection problems, firms prefer to borrow in public debt markets.9
Given these assumptions, the benefit of borrowing from an informed intermediary is increasing in the amount a firm is undervalued in public markets due to its credit ratings. Intuitively, borrowing from an informed intermediary involves greater contracting costs but lower adverse selection costs. We show that for sufficiently high contracting costs, whenEandSagree on a rating, or whenSis more optimistic, only the most undervalued firms will borrow from intermediaries. Additionally, we show that the proportion of firms that find bank borrowing more attractive than borrowing from public markets is greater among firms for whichSassigns a more optimistic rating than among firms for whichSandEagree on the rating.
article.II. Is S&P Overrating a Measure of Ratin:
Both EJR and S&P state in their SEC filings that they issue ordinal through-the-cycle ratings based on the probability of default, and, as shown in TableBIof AppendixB, both use the same ratings scale (AAA to D) and provide virtually identical descriptions of how to interpret their ratings. Moreover, in their SEC filings, both agencies provide similar descriptions of their ratings criteria.10However, since both agencies base their ratings on analyst judgment as well as quantitative models, S&P and EJR ratings may differ for reasons other than incentive conflicts arising from the issuer-paid compensation model. For S&P optimism to be a valid measure of credit quality, we need to show that S&P optimism is related to incentive conflicts and not simply reflective of differences in each agency's true opinion of credit risk.
In this section, we empirically validate S&P overrating as a measure of poor ratings quality using an unbalanced monthly panel of long-term senior issuer ratings pairs from EJR and S&P. We obtain EJR's historical issuer ratings from EJR and S&P's from Compustat.11We exclude financial firms and utilities from our sample, and require that issuers have the necessary Compustat and Mergent data to construct our firm- and bond-level control variables, respectively. Our final sample consists of 68,386 monthly ratings pairs involving 1,023 unique issuers over the period July 1999 through December 2012.
In our sample, S&P and EJR disagree frequently; S&P is more optimistic (pessimistic) than EJR in about 35% (37%) of the firm-months, and the two agencies agree in only about 28% of the firm-months. However, conditional on a disagreement, in 57% (83%) of firm-months EJR and S&P assign ratings within one (two) notch (notches) of each other, indicating that large disagreements are relatively rare. Moreover, when we compare their letter ratings without the ± notch distinctions, we find that they agree in 62% of firm-months in our sample. By way of comparison, Moody's and S&P agree 35% of the time, slightly more frequently than EJR and S&P.
That EJR's ratings can be more optimistic than S&P's indicates that ratings inflation by S&P is not the only reason for ratings differences; for example, ratings differences may also arise if the two agencies use different rating models or assumptions. Below, we provide five sets of empirical results, suggesting that S&P overrating arises in part from S&P incentives to engage in ratings inflation and that it is a valid measure of ratings quality.
If S&P overrating arises from ratings inflation, we would expect overrated firms to be riskier, on average, than non-overrated firms with the same S&P rating (see TableI). To test this conjecture, we regress an S&P overrating indicator on a set of issuer credit risk characteristics that are lagged by one quarter. If S&P overrating reflects random differences of opinion between S&P and EJR, the coefficient estimates on risk characteristics should be indistinguishable from zero. However, as shown in column (1) of TableII, we find that within a given S&P rating category, issuers that are overrated by S&P have significantly higher leverage and lower profitability, on average, than issuers that are not overrated by S&P.12This finding is robust to the inclusion of firm fixed effects (column (2)), which indicates that S&P is more likely to overrate an issuer during times when the issuer has below-average profitability and above-average leverage. To mitigate the concern that these results may be driven by EJR simply updating its ratings faster than S&P when the issuer's default risk increases, in column (3), we also compare EJR and S&P ratings during the month in which EJR initiates ratings coverage. As shown, the first EJR rating is less favorable than the outstanding S&P rating for firms with lower profitability and higher leverage, consistent with the full-sample results. Finally, in columns (4) through (6), we compare the characteristics of firms that are overrated by S&P to the characteristics of firms overrated by EJR. As shown, within a given S&P rating category, firms that are overrated by S&P have significantly higher leverage and lower operating income than firms that are overrated by EJR.
In TableIII, we examine the relation between S&P overrating and the ex-post likelihood of default. We identify future defaults using downgrades by S&P to default (alphanumerical rating “D”) or selective default (alphanumerical rating “SD”), as well as default events (missed interest and principal payments, bankruptcies, and distressed exchanges) recorded by Moody's Default & Recovery Database. In columns (1) and (3), we document that, within an S&P rating category, firms that are overrated by S&P have significantly higher three- and five-year default rates than firms that are not overrated by S&P. The coefficient estimates in TableIIIindicate that firms overrated by S&P have about 2.7% (3.9%) higher default rates within the next three (five) years than non-overrated firms with the same S&P rating. Relative to the unconditional three- (five-) year default rate of 5.2% (7.8%) in our full sample, the higher default rates associated with S&P overrating are economically important. Moreover, as shown in columns (2) and (4), we find no significant difference in the default rates of firms that are underrated by S&P (and thus overrated by EJR) and those that obtained the same rating from EJR and S&P. This result suggests that EJR ratings are incrementally informative about future default rates (i.e., they add to the granularity of S&P ratings) only when S&P ratings are potentially inflated. This asymmetry in the incremental information content of EJR ratings is hard to explain with differences in EJR's and S&P's ratings models and is more consistent with ratings inflation by S&P.
Taken together, the evidence in TablesIIandIIIindicates that both ex-ante and ex-post default risk is significantly higher for firms that are overrated by S&P than for non-overrated firms with the same S&P rating, consistent with S&P overrating serving as a valid indicator of poor ratings quality.
Past studies document that issuer-paid rating agencies tend to provide optimistically biased ratings for frequent bond issuers as well as for issuers with many outstanding bonds, since those firms pay substantially more rating fees than infrequent issuers or issuers with just a few outstanding bonds. Prior evidence also suggests that HY issuers, as well as issuers at the HY-IG boundary, tend to pressure rating agencies and engage in ratings shopping for a higher rating since an incremental rating increase is likely to have a greater effect on bond yields for those firms than for IG firms.13
Following the literature, and to validate S&P overrating as a measure of ratings quality, we examine whether the likelihood of S&P overrating is higher for frequent versus infrequent issuers, issuers with many versus few bond issues outstanding, HY versus IG issuers, and issuers for which the two agencies disagree around the HY-IG boundary versus issuers for which there is no disagreement around the boundary. Specifically, we estimate a series of linear probability models where we regress S&P overrating on a set of lagged firm characteristics as well as indicator variables that proxy for the severity of S&P's incentive conflicts. We define a frequent issuer as a firm that issued an above-median number of public debt securities over the past three years according to Mergent, where the median is conditional on conducting at least one public debt offering over the past three years. We define an issuer with many bond issues outstanding as a firm that has an above-mediannumberof bonds trading in TRACE during the past month, where the median is conditional on at least one bond trading during the past month. We categorize firms as HY based on both agencies assigning an HY rating, and we categorize disagreements around the HY-IG boundary based on one agency assigning an issuer a rating of BBB- or higher, while the other agency assigns the same issuer a rating of BB+ or lower.
As shown in columns (1) and (3) of TableIV, we find that S&P is significantly more likely to overrate frequent issuers, issuers with many outstanding bonds, HY issuers, and issuers around the HY-IG boundary. The coefficient estimates suggest that frequent issuers (issuers with many outstanding bonds) are about 16% (18%) more likely to be overrated than infrequent issuers (issuers with few outstanding bonds), while HY issuers are about 19% more likely to be overrated by S&P than IG issuers. Relative to the frequency of S&P overrating in the full sample (35%), the estimated effects of our incentive conflict measures on the likelihood of overrating are economically large. In columns (2) and (4), we include firm characteristics as well as industry and year fixed effects as additional controls. While the coefficient estimates on our incentive conflict measures become slightly smaller, all of the estimates remain statistically significant and economically important.
While the results in TableIVare consistent with S&P overrating serving as a valid indicator of ratings quality, an alternative explanation is that our incentive conflict proxies are correlated with firm credit risk characteristics (e.g., size) that EJR weighs more heavily than S&P. To mitigate this concern, we examine whether Moody's, which is issuer-paid just like S&P, tends to overrate the same group of issuers that S&P overrates. For this analysis, we compare Moody's ratings to CDS-implied ratings because, unlike S&P or EJR ratings, Moody's ratings are based on expected credit losses and not just the probability of default. As shown in Table IA.I in the Internet Appendix, we find that frequent issuers, issuers with many bonds outstanding, HY issuers, as well as issuers at the HY-IG boundary are all significantly more likely to be overrated by Moody's relative to CDS-implied ratings, which provides additional support to our incentive conflicts story.14
Because of institutional factors that generate significant discontinuities in credit supply around the HY-IG threshold, a one-notch higher rating from S&P that moves an HY issuer to IG status might have a substantial effect on the issuer's cost of borrowing in the bond market. This may lead issuers with IG ratings to pressure S&P to maintain their IG status (e.g., to delay a potential downgrade), and issuers just below the threshold to request an elevated rating. Thus, if conflicts of interest influence S&P's ratings, we would expect S&P to issue a disproportionately low frequency of BB+ ratings and a disproportionately high frequency of BBB− ratings. Consistent with this conjecture, as shown in Figure1, we find that S&P's rating distribution exhibits a “kink” around the HY-IG boundary.15In contrast, we find no such kink in the distribution of EJR's ratings. Using a methodology developed by Jylhä (2011), we find a significantly greater discontinuity in S&P ratings than EJR ratings at the HY-IG boundary (thez-statistic for the difference in discontinuities is 3.74). We also find a significant discontinuity in the likelihood of S&P overrating around the HY-IG boundary using a test proposed by Bollen and Pool (2009). Finally, we run a multivariate regression discontinuity test and find a significant discontinuity at the HY-IG boundary even after controlling for firm characteristics, industry fixed effects, and time fixed effects (see Internet Appendix Table IA.III).

Since ratings models tend to be sticky and because S&P has to consider the reputational costs of maintaining overly optimistic ratings, we expect ratings disagreements that reflect differences in ratings models to be more persistent than disagreements arising from ratings inflation by S&P. We test this conjecture by examining the monthly evolution of 972 EJR and S&P ratings pairs after EJR initiates coverage of an issuer. We focus on EJR coverage initiations because evidence presented by Xia (2014) suggests that EJR's coverage initiations significantly elevate S&P's reputational concerns and reduce the optimism bias in S&P ratings. Because S&P overrating is more likely to reflect ratings inflation than S&P underrating, we expect S&P ratings to be less (more) persistent when S&P is initially more optimistic (pessimistic) than EJR. Since EJR has no incentive to issue inflated ratings, we do not expect the persistence of its ratings to vary with the nature of the initial disagreement with S&P. Finally, we expect the likelihood that S&P (EJR) converges to EJR (S&P) to be higher (lower) when S&P is initially the more optimistic rating agency.
To assess persistence in ratings disagreements, we begin by analyzing how long it takes each rating agency to change its rating from the time EJR initiates coverage using a Cox constant proportion hazard model. The use of a hazard model is motivated by the fact that our data are right censored because not all ratings disagreements are ultimately resolved. Additionally, the hazard model allows us to control for observable firm characteristics at the time of initial ratings coverage that might help determine the speed with which the rating agencies revise their ratings.
TableVpresents our findings. In columns (1) through (3) ((4) through (6)), we model the number of months until S&P (EJR) first revises its initial rating as a function of the gap between S&P and EJR ratings at the time EJR initiates coverage. We report coefficient estimates in exponentiated form in the table. For example, the exponentiated coefficient estimate of 1.468 on S&P overrating in column (1) indicates that S&P updates its rating 1.468 times faster, on average, when EJR's initial rating is less favorable than that of S&P compared to when EJR's initial rating is the same as that of S&P. This result is robust to inclusion of firm characteristics and year fixed effects, as shown in columns (2) and (3). In contrast, as shown in columns (4) through (6), we find no significant relation between the speed of EJR's subsequent ratings adjustments and the nature of the initial disagreement with S&P.
We next examine which rating agency is more likely to converge to the other following an initial ratings disagreement. We do so using two approaches. First, we examine whether S&P or EJR is more likely to converge to the other agency's rating following an initial disagreement. We define S&P as converging toward EJR if the first rating change by S&P after EJR's coverage initiation is a downgrade and S&P initially overrated the firm, or if the first rating change by S&P is an upgrade and S&P initially underrated the firm. Similarly, we define EJR as converging toward S&P if the first rating change by EJR is an upgrade and S&P initially overrated the firm, or if the first rating change by EJR is a downgrade and S&P initially underrated the firm. Note that this test is conditional on a subsequent ratings change occurring. The variable of interest in this analysis is “S&P Overrates at Initiation.” As shown in columns (1) and (2) of TableVI, S&P is about 30% more likely to converge toward EJR when S&P's initial rating is more optimistic than that of EJR (significant at the 1% level). In stark contrast, we find a 13% to 17%lowerlikelihood that EJR converges to S&P when S&P's rating was initially more optimistic than that of EJR (significant at the 1% level), as shown in columns (3) and (4).
A potential criticism of the test above is that it does not account for the fact that both agencies might change their ratings, and hence, ratings disagreements might not be completely resolved, or that one agency might adjust its initial rating by a greater number of notches. Therefore, in our second test, we focus on cases in which the initial ratings disagreement is eventually resolved. We consider the initial disagreement as resolved when EJR and S&P first agree on the rating or when the nature of the disagreement changes (e.g., from overrated to underrated). We then analyze which rating agency adjusts its initial rating by a larger number of notches from the initial rating date until the resolution date. As shown in columns (5) and (6) of TableVI, we find that S&P is between 11% and 19% more likely to adjust its ratings by more notches than EJR, when S&P is initially the more optimistic agency (significant at the 10% level).
Finally, to further address concerns that our proxies for incentives conflicts may be correlated with risk factors and thus not reflect the propensity to inflate ratings, we establish a causal relationship between S&P overrating and incentive conflicts using a quasi-random shock to S&P's incentives to engage in ratings inflation. Fong et al. (2014) find that exogenous reductions in sell-side equity analyst coverage due to brokerage firm mergers increase optimism bias in issuer-paid ratings and reduce rating informativeness. Their argument is that consensus earnings forecasts are, on average, less optimistic and more accurate when more sell-side analysts cover an issuer, which should make it more difficult for a credit rating agency to issue optimistically biased ratings and remain credible.16
If the loss of analyst coverage reduces the quality of issuer-paid ratings, and S&P overrating serves as a valid measure of ratings quality, then the likelihood of S&P overrating should increase after events that lead to exogenous reductions in the number of sell-side analysts covering an issuer. We employ a difference-in-differences research design to test this conjecture. In our estimations, instead of using the brokerage firm mergers in Fong et al. (2014), a large majority of which precede our sample period and some of which are not exogenous according to Kelly and Ljungqvist (2012), we use the brokerage firm merger and closure events listed in AppendixAof Kelly and Ljungqvist (2012).17
As in Kelly and Ljungqvist (2012), our treatment sample consists of (i) firms that lost coverage due to brokerage firm closures, or (ii) in the case of brokerage firm mergers, those firms that were covered by both the target and the acquirer in the year prior to the merger and continued to be covered by the acquiring firm after the merger. We exclude firms that were affected by another merger or closure event in the preceding 12-month period. For each firm in the treatment sample, we find a control firm (with replacement) that has the same EJR and S&P rating as the treated firm at the end of the month preceding the event date and that was not affected by any of our sample brokerage firm closures or mergers during the (−12, +12) calendar-month window centered on the event month. When we find multiple control firms, we select the one that is most similar to the “treated” firm with respect to the number of analysts at the end of the month before the event date. We break any ties by randomly selecting one of the potential control firms. We require firms in both the treatment and the control samples to have S&P and EJR ratings one calendar year after the event month.
Our treatment sample covers 28 events and 275 unique treated firms.18Because some treated firms are affected by multiple broker closures and merger events, this yields 370 firm-events. The control sample consists of 370 firm-events by 201 unique firms. For each firm-event, we obtain the relevant data from the month preceding the event and the year following the event to create our final panel (two observations by firm-event: one for the month before the event and one for the year following the event). We measure post-event credit ratings 12 months after the event date to allow sufficient time for rating agencies to adjust their ratings.
TableVIIreports results of our difference-in-differences analysis. The dependent variable equals 1 if S&P overrates the issuer and 0 otherwise. In column (1), we present a parsimonious model that includes the dummy variablesTreatmentandPost, as well as the interaction term between the two variables. The insignificant coefficient estimate onTreatmentin this column indicates that no significant difference exists in the frequency of S&P overrating between the treatment and control groups in the pre-event period. The insignificant coefficient estimate onPostindicates that the likelihood of S&P overrating does not change for the control group in the post-event period. Finally, the coefficient estimate on the interaction term is 0.124 (significant at the 1% level), indicating that the frequency of S&P overrating increases 12.4% more for the treatment sample than for the control sample in the post-event period.
In column (2), we control for the number of analysts that covered the firm before the event as well as for event fixed effects. In column (3), we add contemporaneous firm-level controls, industry fixed effects, as well as fixed effects for the S&P rating before the event as additional controls. The inclusion of additional controls reduces the average treatment effect slightly from 12.4% to 12.1% but does not affect its significance level. Because our treatment and control firms can enter the sample multiple times, we are also able to estimate models with firm fixed effects to remove any time-invariant unobserved heterogeneity between treatment and control firms that could influence the likelihood of overrating. As shown in columns (4) through (6), the inclusion of firm fixed effects has no discernible effect on the size or significance of our key coefficient estimate. Overall, the findings in TableVIIare consistent with S&P overrating serving as a valid measure of poor ratings quality.19
We believe that the results presented so far strongly support the view that S&P overrating arises in part from ratings inflation and that S&P overrating is a valid measure of ratings quality. In the following sections, we examine how concerns about ratings inflation impact bond prices and firms’ choice between private debt and “arm's length” debt.
article.III. Ratings Quality and Secondary Marke:
In this section, we examine whether investors recognize and price poor-quality issuer-paid ratings in secondary bond markets. We employ two identification strategies. First, based on the model developed in SectionI, we pool bond-month observations for which there is either no or one-notch disagreement between S&P and EJR ratings and compare the average bond spreads of issuers overrated by S&P with those of issuers overrated by EJR. The idea is to test whether investors put less weight on optimistic S&P ratings than on optimistic EJR ratings, holding the average rating constant. Second, we use an exogenous change in S&P's incentives to provide inflated ratings to identify the causal effect of S&P overrating on corporate bond yields.
The model outlined in SectionIillustrates that if investors are concerned about ratings inflation when S&P rates an issuer more favorably than EJR and adjust bond prices accordingly, yield spreads for split-rated bonds should be different depending on whether EJR or S&P assigns the more optimistic rating. Motivated by our model, in this section, we empirically examine the yield-spread differences of split-rated bonds by pooling a large sample of fixed-rate nonconvertible bond transactions between July 2002 and December 2012 from the Enhanced TRACE database.20Our pricing analysis focuses on yield spreads in the secondary bond market to avoid potential selection issues. Because EJR only assigns senior issuer ratings, we only include senior bonds in our sample for which the S&P issue-level rating is equal to the S&P issuer rating. We calculate daily average bond yield spreads over the corresponding Treasury rate by weighing each bond transaction by its size (Bessembinder et al. (2009)). For each bond, we then average the daily yield spreads across the calendar month. We restrict the sample to bond-month observations for which we have both an EJR and an S&P rating and for which all relevant firm- and bond-level controls from Compustat and Mergent are available. Finally, since our goal is to compare bond prices associated with symmetric ratings pairs, for example, (S&P = BB+, EJR = BB) versus (S&P = BB, EJR = BB+), which are hard to find when the difference between S&P and EJR ratings exceeds one notch, we restrict the sample to instances in which EJR and S&P either agree or disagree by only one notch. Our final sample is an unbalanced panel data set of over 122,000 monthly bond spreads of 6,351 unique issues by 396 unique firms. The majority of bonds that are traded are rated IG, and only about 13% of the issues in our sample have a HY rating.

Turning to our main independent variables,ISplitis an indicator variable that takes a value of 1 if S&P and EJR disagree and 0 if they agree,IS&POveris an indicator variable that takes a value of 1 if S&P overrates and 0 otherwise, andIBestratingandIWorstratingare indicators for the best and worst ratings between the two agencies, respectively. Finally,Zis a matrix of issuer and bond characteristics andvtrepresents year-month fixed effects.21
Equation4allows the effect of S&P overrating on bond spreads to vary with the issuer rating. Specifically, it includes the interaction betweenIS&POverand an indicator for whether EJR and S&P disagree across the HY-IG boundary (IHYB) as well as an indicator for whether both EJR and S&P rate the issuer HY (IHY). The idea is to examine the effect of S&P overrating separately where inflated ratings are likely to have the greatest effect on bond prices. Note that right over the HY-IG boundary, any yield premium that investors demand for holding overrated bonds might be offset by increased demand for bonds with an IG rating, making it difficult to identify the effect of ratings quality on bond spreads.
TableVIIIpresents our estimation results. In columns (1) through (4) (columns (5) through (8)), our models include indicators for the best (worst) of the two ratings. As shown in column (1), consistent with our model, we find that split-rated bonds within the HY segment have yield spreads that are, on average, about 58 basis points higher when S&P overrates than when EJR overrates (significant at the 5% level). We find a similar effect in column (4) when we replace superior rating indicators with inferior rating indicators.22In contrast, for IG bonds, we find that S&P overrating is associated with a small discount in yields.
To examine whether the yield premium associated with optimistic S&P ratings is related to the severity of potential incentive conflicts, we next divide our sample based on whether the firm is a frequent issuer and whether the issuer has many outstanding bonds. For firms at the HY-IG boundary and HY firms that are frequent issuers or issuers with many outstanding bonds, we find evidence consistent with investors recognizing S&P's incentives to issue inflated ratings. For example, as shown in columns (2), (3), (6), and (7), for frequent HY issuers and HY issuers with many outstanding bonds, the yield premium for optimistic S&P ratings over optimistic EJR ratings is between 89 and 102 basis points and higher than the premium for the full sample of HY firms. Moreover, for frequent issuers and issuers with many outstanding bonds at the HY-IG boundary, there is a significant yield premium of between 61 and 66 basis points when the firm is rated IG by S&P and HY by EJR than when the firm is rated IG by EJR and HY by S&P. As shown in columns (4) and (8), we find no evidence of a yield premium for overrated firms that are infrequent issuers with few outstanding bonds.23Indeed, we find a significant yield discount for IG firms that are infrequent issuers with few outstanding bonds.
Overall, the evidence presented in TableVIIIsuggests that for HY issuers and issuers at the HY-IG boundary, investors place significantly less weight on optimistic S&P ratings relative to optimistic EJR ratings. Taken together with the argument that S&P's incentives to engage in ratings inflation are greater for these issuers, the evidence in TableVIIIis consistent with the view that investors discount optimistic S&P ratings, at least in part, due to conflicts of interest.
In Section II.D, we provide evidence that the propensity for S&P to issue more optimistic ratings than EJR increases after issuers suffer exogenous losses of sell-side stock analyst coverage. In this subsection, we use the increase in S&P overrating after those events to identify the causal effect of ratings inflation on corporate bond yields. Specifically, we implement a two-stage least squares (2SLS) estimation strategy where we usePost×Treatmentas an instrument for S&P overrating in the first stage, and we regress secondary market bond spreads on the predicted value of S&P overrating in the second stage.24In addition to the instrument, our first-stage model includes the indicatorsPostandTreatmentas well as all of the second-stage regressors.
Our estimation sample consists of secondary bond market yield spreads during the (−12, +12) calendar months around each broker closure and merger event (excluding the event month) for a ratings-matched sample of treatment and control firms. Because broker closures and mergers are firm-level events, in this analysis, we aggregate bond spreads at the firm level if an issuer has more than one senior bond trading in TRACE in a given month, to avoid double-counting monthly observations for the same firm. We do so by calculating the value-weighted average yield spread across all senior bonds using the remaining outstanding amounts as weights. In our analysis, we require at least one month of TRACE data before and after the event month for both our treatment and our control firms. Because TRACE coverage began in July 2002, our sample here is smaller than that used in TableVII; specifically, the sample here consists of 4,580 firm-months around 210 firm-events that involve 105 unique treatment firms and 70 unique control firms.
TableIXpresents the second-stage yield spread regressions. As shown in column (1), controlling for firm, time, and event fixed effects as well as time-varying firm financial characteristics and S&P rating dummies, we find a positive and significant relation between credit spreads and the instrumented value of S&P overrating. This result suggests that investors recognize and discount inflated S&P ratings: that is, they require a significant yield premium on bonds overrated by S&P.
In column (2), we investigate whether the effect of S&P overrating on bond spreads is greater for HY firms and firms at the HY-IG boundary, relative to IG firms, by including interaction terms between S&P overrating and binary HY and HY-IG boundary variables. In these specifications, we usePost×Treatment×HY-IG boundaryandPost×Treatment × HY, respectively, as additional instruments. As shown, the effect of S&P overrating on yield spreads is significantly higher for HY firms as well as firms at the HY-IG boundary, relative to IG firms, consistent with the evidence based on reduced-form credit spread models in TableVIII.25
article.IV. Ratings Quality and the Choice betwe:
Our model in SectionIillustrates how poor-quality ratings can result in the pooling of diverse credit risks within a rating category, and increase information asymmetries between issuers and bond investors. As a result, investors will demand higher yields in cases in which they are more concerned about inflated ratings, which will lead to undervaluation of relatively low credit risk firms within a given rating category in public bond markets.
When issuers have the option to borrow from a well-informed intermediary, such as a bank, which can more accurately assess their credit risk than observed ratings, they can mitigate potential undervaluation in the public bond market by substituting intermediated debt for public debt. However, to the extent that borrowing from an informed intermediary involves higher incremental contracting costs, only the most undervalued firms will find this option attractive. In the context of our model, the proportion of firms that are most adversely affected by undervaluation in the bond market is greatest in cases in which the issuer-paid agency assigns a more optimistic rating, and therefore, conditional on raising external debt financing, the likelihood of private borrowing will be positively related to whether a firm is overrated by the issuer-paid rating agency at the time of its debt offering.
Our model also implies that borrowing from an informed intermediary will reveal overrated firms to be undervalued by the bond market and therefore should reduce the asymmetric information discount in the bond market associated with optmistic issuer-paid ratings. We test these implications using a monthly panel data set of private and public debt issuances from Dealscan and Mergent.
We begin by examining how ratings quality affects the choice between informed debt (e.g., bank debt) and “arm's length” public debt financing. We identify movements in the bond supply based on mispricing following the empirical approach in Becker and Ivashina (2014). Specifically, we examine the substitution between private debt and public debt conditional on the issuance of new debt. By revealed preferences, firms that obtain new debt financing must have a positive demand for debt. We interpret a substitution of private debt for public debt by firms that are overrated by S&P as evidence of a shift in the bond credit supply based on poor-quality ratings.
We identify months in which firms borrow from private lenders as months in which they issue a term loan (from Dealscan) or a nonconvertible private debt security (from Mergent). Months in which firms borrow from public markets are defined as months in which they issue a nonconvertible public bond (from Mergent). Following Becker and Ivashina (2014), we exclude months in which firms issue both private and public debt. Our estimation sample is an unbalanced monthly panel data set of 1,459 public and 1,306 private debt issuances by 651 unique firms.
TableXpresents the results. In column (1), we find that firms that are overrated by S&P are 5.5% more likely to issue private debt than firms that are not overrated by S&P (significant at the 1% level). In column (2), we do not find a significant difference in the likelihood of issuing private debt for firms that are underrated by S&P and firms that are rated the same by S&P and EJR. To ensure that unobserved time-invariant differences in the firm characteristics of overrated and non-overrated firms do not drive these results, we estimate regressions with firm fixed effects and report our findings in columns (3) and (4). As can be seen, the coefficient estimate on S&P overrating remains economically important and statistically significant in the firm fixed effects specifications.
While the results in TableXare consistent with the implications of our model, a potential concern with our identification strategy is that, in our sample, firms rated A− or higher rarely issue private debt, whereas firms rated B− or lower rarely issue nonconvertible public debt. To address this concern, we estimate the issuance models using the subsample of firms that are rated between BBB+ and BB− by S&P in the month before debt issuance. We choose this range of ratings for two reasons. First, it ensures the greatest overlap between firms that issue public and private debt. Specifically, about 61% of firm-months involving private issues are by firms rated between BBB+ and BB− by S&P, compared to 52% of firm-months involving public debt issues. Second, as shown in TableIV, overrating by S&P is more prevalent in HY firms and firms around the HY-IG boundary. Moreover, these firms may have greater incentives to borrow from informed private lenders than from public markets. As shown in the Internet Appendix to this paper, the results are similar to those in TableXwhen we restrict our sample to firms rated between BBB+ and BB− by S&P.26The inclusion of firm fixed effects in TableXshould help mitigate concerns about omitted variables bias. Nevertheless, as a robustness check, we also estimate issuance choice regressions using an instrumental variables approach similar to those described in Section III.B. The results are consistent with those reported in TableXand are presented in the Internet Appendix.27
In this section, we examine whether borrowing from informed intermediaries reduces the asymmetric information discount in the secondary bond market for overrated firms.
In our first test, we estimate difference-in-differences regressions to assess the effect of private debt offerings on the firm-level yield premium associated with S&P overrating. Our estimation sample consists of firms that were overrated by S&P at the end of the month preceding the issuance (treatment group) and firms that were not overrated by S&P (control group). The event window is the (−3, +3) calendar-months centered on the issuance month. We drop the event month from our estimation sample. Also, to mitigate potential confounding effects of preceding debt offerings, we exclude firms that issued any private debt over the (−6, −1) month window.28Moreover, to isolate the effect of changes in ratings quality from the effect of ratings revisions, we focus on cases in which neither S&P nor EJR updated their ratings during our estimation window. Finally, we require at least one month of TRACE data both before and after the event month for both our treatment and our control firms. Our treatment (control) sample consists of 71 (114) private debt issues by 54 (90) unique issuers. Taken together, this yields a sample of 1,015 firm-months. Because TRACE coverage began only in July 2002, our bond pricing sample is smaller than the sample that we use in our issuance choice analysis in Section IV.A.

The main coefficient of interest in equation5is α2, which measures the average change in the yield premium associated with S&P overrating following the issuance of private debt. If borrowing from a well-informed intermediary reduces asymmetric information costs associated with lower quality ratings, we should find a negative and statistically significant coefficient estimate on α2.
TableXIpresents the results. As shown in columns (1) and (2), we find that spreads for overrated firms decline by between 62 and 67 basis points after private debt offerings (significant at the 1% level). Relative to the average yield spread of 410 basis points for overrated firms prior to issuance, this represents a reduction of about 15%. As shown in columns (3) and (4), the inclusion of firm fixed effects has no discernible effect on the size or significance of the coefficient estimate on α2, mitigating concerns about omitted variable bias.
While the results in TableXIare consistent with issuance of private debt revealing positive private information about the credit quality of issuers, a potential concern is that the sample is limited to firms that issue private debt and does not allow us to control for the potentially similar informational content of public bond issuances. In the context of our model, only the most undervalued firms will borrow from informed intermediaries since contracting costs are greater for private debt than for public debt. As a result, we expect firms with low-quality ratings that issue private debt to see greater reductions in their bond spreads after issuance compared to firms that issue public debt.

TableXIIpresents the results. As can be seen, for firms that are overrated by S&P, we find significant declines in bond spreads following both private and public debt issuances. The average reduction is between 80 and 87 basis points after private debt offerings (significant at the 1% level) and between 31 and 34 basis points following public debt offerings (significant at the 1% level). These results are not sensitive to the inclusion of firm and bond characteristics as well as firm and year-quarter fixed effects. For firms that are not overrated by S&P, we find that the average spread change following public debt offerings is not statistically indistinguishable from the average spread change following private issuances (as measured by α7).
Overall, the evidence presented in TablesXIandXIIis consistent with issuance of private debt reducing the asymmetric information discount associated with poor-quality ratings in secondary bond markets.
article.V. Conclusion:
Credit rating agencies facilitate “arm's length” debt financing by mitigating information asymmetries between issuers and investors. In this paper, we investigate how the quality of issuer-paid ratings affects corporate bond yields and firms’ choice between “arm's length” debt and intermediated debt. We provide evidence that even though they are priced in the bond market, poor-quality ratings exacerbate information asymmetries and affect firms’ choice of borrowing venue. Our analysis provides further evidence that information asymmetries significantly affect firms’ financing choices (see, for example, Tang (2009) and Faulkender and Petersen (2006)). Specifically, our results suggest that firms with poor-quality ratings shift their reliance from rating agencies as an information intermediary to banks and other private lenders. Our analysis also suggests that incentive conflicts reduce investor confidence in issuer-paid ratings when those ratings are more optimistic than investor-paid ratings or market-based indicators of credit risk.

article.Appendix A: Model on the Effect of Ratin:
In this appendix, we present a simple model in which both private lenders and rating agencies are assumed to have the ability to mitigate adverse selection problems in credit markets. The model is intended to illustrate (i) the conditions under which borrowing from private lenders conveys information about a firm's credit quality, (ii) how optimism in issuer-paid ratings can lead overrated firms to borrow from private debt markets instead of from public debt markets, and (iii) the conditions under which ratings continue to be informative about credit risk when borrowers have the option to borrow from a well-informed private lender.
The model is based on the assumption that private lenders are able to provide a more accurate assessment of the probability of default than rating agencies. We incorporate this feature into the model by assuming that private lenders and managers observe the issuer's “true” credit risk, whereas rating agencies provide a noisy (but informative) assessment of credit risk.
The structure of the model is similar to the model developed by Hadlock and James (2002) concerning the choice between bank debt and public debt. We assume that there areNdifferent risk categories and that there arenfirms uniformly distributed across these risk categories. Firms in a given risk categoryihave assets in place that yield a terminal payoff ofAhin the high state orAlin the low state, whereAh>Al>0. Firms must raiseIfor an investment project that paysRin the high state and zero in the low state. The probability of the low state for firms in risk categoryiisDi. We assume thatRis sufficiently high so that firms will always invest. Investors are risk-neutral and there is no discounting. Firms choose to fund the project with either public debt or private debt. Given these assumptions, public debt (private debt) is sold at its expected value conditional on the public's (the private lender's) information. We assume thatAl<I, so that a firm that borrows is sure to default in the low state. Both the firm and the bank are assumed to knowDi.
The cost of private borrowing is assumed to differ from the cost of borrowing in the public markets and is lower for some types of firms than for others. For example, issuance costs for public debt may be relatively higher for small firms with a limited track record or firms with valuable growth opportunities. Distress costs, monitoring costs, or incentive conflicts between shareholders and debtholders may also vary with the type of borrowing and the characteristics of the firm. We follow Hadlock and James (2002) and model these potential differences in transaction costs and agency costs by assuming that in the event that the firm uses private debt (public debt), the various contracting costs that arise reduce the total cash flows of the issuer in the low state by the amountCB(CP). We also assume that all parties know the contracting costs that a given firm might face.
Assume initially that both rating agencies follow the same rating scale and have the same sorting skills (in the sense that the frequency with which they underrate and overrate issuers is the same). For simplicity, assume that bothεi,Sandεi,Eare independent of one another, are symmetrically distributed, and can take on values of 0 and±ΔD, such that the change in default probability across rating categories is the same. Given these assumptions, ratings are unbiased estimates of true credit risk (i.e.,E[εi,S]=E[εi,E]=0). Finally, assume that the likelihood that a firm with credit riskiis assigned the correct rating is ρ, withρ≥13.
In the discussion that follows, we focus on the borrowing decisions of firms in a given risk category. We assume that the risk category is not at a boundary (i.e., the lowest or highest possible risk). We defineDi+1as the credit risk associated with the next-worse credit rating(Di+ΔD)andDi−1as the credit risk associated with the next-best credit rating(Di−ΔD).

Whether firms will choose to issue private or public debt will depend on their true credit risk, the relative magnitude of the contracting costs, the perceived accuracy of the rating agencies, as well as what investors infer about their true credit risk once they make their choice.
IfCB<CP, all firms will borrow from private lenders because both contracting and adverse selection costs are lower when firms borrow privately. In the case of ratings agreement(Ri,E=Ri,S=Di), typeDi−1firms will always be better off borrowing from private lenders even if the market correctly assesses their credit risk.29Because typeDi−1firms will always issue private debt, typeDifirms will also always issue private debt, as issuing public debt will either reveal their true credit quality or investors will pool typesDiandDi+1together, which results in an increased expected probability of default and borrowing cost for firms of typeDi. Finally, typeDi+1firms will also borrow from private lenders, as not doing so would reveal their true credit quality and increase their borrowing costs becauseCB<CP. In the case of split ratings(Ri,E=Di+1,Ri,s=Di), the reasoning is similar.
In the case in whichCB>CP, which firms borrow publicly and which firms borrow privately depends, in part, on the difference in the magnitude of contracting costs between the public and private markets, as well as on the accuracy of the rating agencies. For ratings to be informative (that is for ratings to affect investors’ assessment of credit risk in the primary bond market), private contracting costs must be at least large enough such that both typeDiand typeDi+1firms find private borrowing unattractive conditional on ratings agreement. To understand why, first note that conditional on ratings agreement, firms of typeDi+1will always find private borrowing unattractive whenCB>CP. Now consider the case in whichCB>CPbut the difference between contracting costs is sufficiently small that, conditional on ratings agreement, both typeDi−1and typeDifirms find private borrowing attractive. Then, typeDifirms with split ratings will also find private borrowing more attractive.30This would imply that ratings are no longer informative because investors would expect firms with split ratings and firms with ratings agreement that do not borrow from private lenders to be of typeDi+1and require the same yield.

If both rating agencies have similar skill in identifying firms’ true credit risk but one of the agencies has a propensity to inflate its ratings due to incentive conflicts, then the general relationship in equationA1will not hold. Instead, investors’ inference about the expected default probability will be a function of the rating agencies’ skills and the proportion of firms with misreported ratings. Consider the case in which the issuer-paid rating agencySinflates its ratings for some firms based on factors unobservable to investors (such as future business opportunities or future fee income). Investors know thatSinflates its ratings for some firms, and they may believe that the propensity to inflate is related to observable firm characteristics, but we assume that they do not know with certainty whether the rating for a given firm is inflated. For example, investors may believe that frequent past issuers, firms with many outstanding bonds, or firms at the HY-IG boundary are more likely to be overrated by agencyS, but they do not know which firms have inflated ratings.

As in the case of no inflation, the nature of possible equilibria with ratings inflation will depend, in part, on the difference in the magnitude of contracting costs between the public and private debt markets. In the case in which contracting costs in the public debt market exceed the costs in the private marketCB<CP, all firms will borrow from private lenders and ratings are uninformative. In the case in whichCB>CP, contracting costs have to be large enough that split ratings are informative.
